brief:
Campaign overview: Harvest Table is running a reel campaign in the restaurant category, and this opportunity is intended to drive qualified inbound creator applications. The selected creator should build work that feels native to Instagram, with obvious relevance to people who already engage with creator-led recommendations. We want the creative to feel commercially sharp while still reflecting the style and decision-making patterns that make influencer content persuasive. This is not a generic awareness brief. The campaign needs content that makes the product, offer, or experience understandable within seconds and worth remembering after the first view.

Creative objective: the asset should communicate why Harvest Table deserves attention now, not later. That means the first moments should establish context quickly, the middle should show substance, and the ending should create a rational next step for the audience. We are looking for creators who understand how to translate brand asks into platform-specific storytelling rather than simply reading benefit points. The best submissions will combine a strong hook, visible product or service integration, and enough real-world texture to make the recommendation believable. Every creative choice should support clarity, momentum, and purchase or inquiry intent.

Deliverables: we expect a primary hero asset with a strong opening hook. If the format allows multiple scenes, the creator should structure them so each moment does distinct work instead of repeating the same message. For a reel, that may mean hook, demonstration, proof, and close. For a post, it may mean lead visual, supporting detail, caption depth, and explicit positioning. For a story, it may mean short linked frames that move naturally from attention to explanation to action. Whatever the execution, the audience should leave with a concrete understanding of the offer, the setting, and why this campaign is relevant to them.

Audience and messaging: the content should speak to young professionals who follow practical recommendations. Messaging should remain confident and polished without sounding overproduced. Avoid writing or performing as if the audience needs to be sold with noise alone. Specificity is more valuable than hype. Use observations that make the experience tangible, such as what stands out about the service, why the workflow is easier, what product detail matters, or how the brand fits into a real routine. The creative should make it obvious that the creator paid attention to the actual product or experience. We want messaging that earns trust through detail instead of relying on generic enthusiasm.

Production guidance: the work should include balanced natural lighting, clean audio, and legible captions when relevant. Visuals need to show real usage or a convincing encounter with the brand rather than disconnected beauty shots. When captions are used, they should add useful information rather than restating the spoken lines word for word. Audio, pacing, and shot selection should all support comprehension on a first pass. If a creator uses trending structure or editing patterns, they should do so selectively and only when it improves retention or clarity. The finished piece should look deliberate, not improvised without a plan.

Brand fit and restrictions: avoid generic praise with no proof points. Keep the brand presence integrated into the story so the recommendation lands naturally. We are not looking for satire, detached irony, or content that treats the brief as a meme unless that direction is explicitly justified by the creator's audience fit and concept. The stronger submissions will show judgment around what to emphasize and what to leave out. If there are multiple angles available, prioritize the one most likely to create action among the right viewers rather than the one that simply generates vanity engagement.

Performance expectations: success for this campaign will be indicated by strong first-three-second retention. The creative therefore needs a structure that respects how people decide on social platforms. Strong hooks matter, but so does the follow-through after attention is captured. We want a viewer to understand what is being shown, why it matters, and what they should do next. Metrics are important, but so is fit. A smaller creator with unusually strong category alignment can outperform a larger creator whose audience is indifferent. The brief should be interpreted with commercial discipline rather than as a pure aesthetics exercise.

Workflow and submission note: creators bidding on this job should assume a fast operating cadence and should move quickly after selection because the reverse-auction window is part of the operating model. Include a short concept summary with the bid so we can evaluate not just style, but strategic understanding. If selected, the creator should be prepared to confirm timing, outline the intended structure, and move into production without unnecessary delays. The final output should feel execution-ready, platform-aware, and grounded in real consumer decision-making. We prefer work that makes the brief easier to approve because the thinking is visible in the content itself.

The final output should feel execution-ready, platform-aware, and grounded in real consumer decision-making. Audience and messaging: the content should speak to followers who respond well to useful, specific creator commentary. Production guidance: the work should include visual continuity that makes the content easy to watch on a phone screen. If a creator uses trending structure or editing patterns, they should do so selectively and only when it improves retention or clarity. The finished piece should look deliberate, not improvised without a plan.

The stronger submissions will show judgment around what to emphasize and what to leave out. We are looking for creators who understand how to translate brand asks into platform-specific storytelling rather than simply reading benefit points. The best submissions will combine a strong hook, visible product or service integration, and enough real-world texture to make the recommendation believable. Every creative choice should support clarity, momentum, and purchase or inquiry intent.

Deliverables: we expect a closing call to action that feels specific rather than generic. If the format allows multiple scenes, the creator should structure them so each moment does distinct work instead of repeating the same message. For a reel, that may mean hook, demonstration, proof, and close. For a post, it may mean lead visual, supporting detail, caption depth, and explicit positioning. For a story, it may mean short linked frames that move naturally from attention to explanation to action. Whatever the execution, the audience should leave with a concrete understanding of the offer, the setting, and why this campaign is relevant to them.

Audience and messaging: the content should speak to followers who respond well to useful, specific creator commentary. Messaging should remain clear enough for conversion while still feeling personal. Avoid writing or performing as if the audience needs to be sold with noise alone. Specificity is more valuable than hype. Use observations that make the experience tangible, such as what stands out about the service, why the workflow is easier, what product detail matters, or how the brand fits into a real routine. The creative should make it obvious that the creator paid attention to the actual product or experience. We want messaging that earns trust through detail instead of relying on generic enthusiasm.

Production guidance: the work should include visual continuity that makes the content easy to watch on a phone screen. Visuals need to show real usage or a convincing encounter with the brand rather than disconnected beauty shots. When captions are used, they should add useful information rather than restating the spoken lines word for word. Audio, pacing, and shot selection should all support comprehension on a first pass. If a creator uses trending structure or editing patterns, they should do so selectively and only when it improves retention or clarity. The finished piece should look deliberate, not improvised without a plan.

Brand fit and restrictions: avoid filler shots that add time but not clarity. Keep the brand presence integrated into the story so the recommendation lands naturally. We are not looking for satire, detached irony, or content that treats the brief as a meme unless that direction is explicitly justified by the creator's audience fit and concept. The stronger submissions will show judgment around what to emphasize and what to leave out. If there are multiple angles available, prioritize the one most likely to create action among the right viewers rather than the one that simply generates vanity engagement.

Performance expectations: success for this campaign will be indicated by a clear rise in click-through or profile actions tied to the post. The creative therefore needs a structure that respects how people decide on social platforms. Strong hooks matter, but so does the follow-through after attention is captured. We want a viewer to understand what is being shown, why it matters, and what they should do next. Metrics are important, but so is fit. A smaller creator with unusually strong category alignment can outperform a larger creator whose audience is indifferent. The brief should be interpreted with commercial discipline rather than as a pure aesthetics exercise.

Workflow and submission note: creators bidding on this job should assume a fast operating cadence and should structure the content so small edits can be made without rebuilding the entire asset. Include a short concept summary with the bid so we can evaluate not just style, but strategic understanding. If selected, the creator should be prepared to confirm timing, outline the intended structure, and move into production without unnecessary delays. The final output should feel execution-ready, platform-aware, and grounded in real consumer decision-making. We prefer work that makes the brief easier to approve because the thinking is visible in the content itself.

The finished piece should look deliberate, not improvised without a plan.

Campaign overview: Pilot Works is running a reel campaign in the restaurant category, and this opportunity is intended to generate measurable platform-native engagement. The selected creator should build work that feels native to Instagram, with obvious relevance to people who already engage with creator-led recommendations. We want the creative to feel commercially sharp while still reflecting the style and decision-making patterns that make influencer content persuasive. This is not a generic awareness brief. The campaign needs content that makes the product, offer, or experience understandable within seconds and worth remembering after the first view.

Creative objective: the asset should communicate why Pilot Works deserves attention now, not later. That means the first moments should establish context quickly, the middle should show substance, and the ending should create a rational next step for the audience. We are looking for creators who understand how to translate brand asks into platform-specific storytelling rather than simply reading benefit points. The best submissions will combine a strong hook, visible product or service integration, and enough real-world texture to make the recommendation believable. Every creative choice should support clarity, momentum, and purchase or inquiry intent.

Deliverables: we expect spoken or written messaging that lands the key value proposition. If the format allows multiple scenes, the creator should structure them so each moment does distinct work instead of repeating the same message. For a reel, that may mean hook, demonstration, proof, and close. For a post, it may mean lead visual, supporting detail, caption depth, and explicit positioning. For a story, it may mean short linked frames that move naturally from attention to explanation to action. Whatever the execution, the audience should leave with a concrete understanding of the offer, the setting, and why this campaign is relevant to them.

Audience and messaging: the content should speak to people already shopping within this category but waiting for a trusted signal. Messaging should remain native to the platform rather than adapted from another channel. Avoid writing or performing as if the audience needs to be sold with noise alone. Specificity is more valuable than hype. Use observations that make the experience tangible, such as what stands out about the service, why the workflow is easier, what product detail matters, or how the brand fits into a real routine. The creative should make it obvious that the creator paid attention to the actual product or experience. We want messaging that earns trust through detail instead of relying on generic enthusiasm.

Production guidance: the work should include clean transitions, no rushed pacing, and a deliberate structure from hook to payoff. Visuals need to show real usage or a convincing encounter with the brand rather than disconnected beauty shots. When captions are used, they should add useful information rather than restating the spoken lines word for word. Audio, pacing, and shot selection should all support comprehension on a first pass. If a creator uses trending structure or editing patterns, they should do so selectively and only when it improves retention or clarity. The finished piece should look deliberate, not improvised without a plan.

Brand fit and restrictions: keep brand mentions integrated into the story instead of making the piece feel like a hard ad. Keep the brand presence integrated into the story so the recommendation lands naturally. We are not looking for satire, detached irony, or content that treats the brief as a meme unless that direction is explicitly justified by the creator's audience fit and concept. The stronger submissions will show judgment around what to emphasize and what to leave out. If there are multiple angles available, prioritize the one most likely to create action among the right viewers rather than the one that simply generates vanity engagement.
